cell.rationale: Retrieved clauses point to different outcomes for the same facts.
No evidence makes the decisive truth unrecoverable.
Review technology existed and the view was clear at the moment of the call.
Named sources frame the same settled fact in opposed valence.

pg-framing-aggrieved.attribution: Manager of the penalised side, post-match interview (2025-10-01)
pg-framing-aggrieved.extracted_text: Manager of the penalised side (2025-10-01) — CONDEMNS: The manager ruled against insisted the contact was a clear, obvious foul that any consistent application of the Law would punish, pointing to other matches where the same contact was given.

pg-framing-benefiting.attribution: Manager of the benefiting side, post-match interview (2025-10-01)
pg-framing-benefiting.extracted_text: Manager of the benefiting side (2025-10-01) — JUSTIFIES: The manager who benefited called the same contact part of the game and praised VAR for not intervening on a subjective matter — reading the identical, agreed contact in the opposite direction.

pg-hist-opposite-rulings.attribution: Curated historical record — The 'subjective' VAR call
pg-hist-opposite-rulings.extracted_text: Across the 2025/26 season, near-identical situations were ruled in opposite ways in different matches — a penalty for minimal contact one week, a clear block waved away the next — and the Key Match Incidents Panel split on several, recording them as "subjective" rather than as errors.

pg-hist-threshold.attribution: Curated historical record — The 'subjective' VAR call
pg-hist-threshold.extracted_text: VAR intervenes only for a "clear and obvious" error, and fouls such as holding or blocking at a set-piece turn on whether the contact is a "non-footballing action". These thresholds are matters of degree, not bright lines, and reasonable officials apply them differently to similar contact.

pg-hist-visible.attribution: Curated historical record — The 'subjective' VAR call
pg-hist-visible.extracted_text: The contact in these incidents was visible on replay and reviewed by VAR; the dispute is not about what happened but about whether the threshold was met. The decision-time information was adequate.

output.rationale: The retrieved record shows the 'clear and obvious' and 'non-footballing action' thresholds are matters of degree, not bright lines, so near-identical contact is ruled in opposite ways across matches (pg-hist-threshold, pg-hist-opposite-rulings); the rule's threshold itself generates the disagreement.
No event-data anomaly resolves whether the threshold was met.

The retrieved facts state the contact was visible on replay and reviewed by VAR (pg-hist-visible); the information available was adequate, so the dispute is not about what could be seen but about whether the threshold was met.

The manager ruled against is reported to call the contact a clear, obvious foul (pg-framing-aggrieved) while the manager who benefited calls the identical contact part of the game (pg-framing-benefiting); two named sources frame the same agreed contact in opposite valence.

settled_fact.statement: Across the current Premier League season, near-identical fouls — holding at a corner, minimal contact on a goalkeeper — are penalised in one match and waved away in the next. Many are logged not as errors but as 'subjective decisions'. The contact is agreed and visible; what splits the room is whether the Law's threshold was met, and which side you support.

cells.rationale: Retrieved clauses point to different outcomes for the same facts.
No evidence makes the decisive truth unrecoverable.
Review technology existed and the view was clear at the moment of the call.
Named sources frame the same settled fact in opposed valence.

split.headline: It stays contested over whether the Law is clear and how named sources frame the same fact — not over the dimensions ruled out.
